 {"id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.5","title":"Suggestions DB: safe alternatives + how-to-allow guidance","description":"## Objective

Make `dcg` helpful, not just restrictive, by attaching curated suggestions to blocked rules.

When a command is blocked (or warned), the user should immediately see:

- what happened (stable rule id)
- why it's risky
- safe alternatives / preview steps
- how to allow safely (rule-id allowlist command)

## Key idea

Maintain a small, explicit "suggestions database" keyed by stable rule id:

- `rule_id = "pack_id:pattern_name"`

Each rule can have 0..N suggestions:

- `preview_first` (e.g., `git clean -n`, `docker system df`)
- `safer_alternative` (e.g., `--force-with-lease`)
- `workflow_fix` (e.g., `git stash` first)
- `allow_safely` (copy/paste allowlist command)
- `docs` (README anchor)

## Why this is worth doing

- Reduces "rage disable" moments.
- Teaches users safer habits.
- Makes false positives much cheaper to resolve.

## Constraints

- Suggestions must be non-prescriptive when environment-specific.
- Suggestions must be safe-by-default (no destructive commands suggested).
- Hook output must remain bounded in size.

## Acceptance criteria

- For core rules, deny output includes at least one actionable suggestion.
- Explain mode includes suggestions in all formats.
- Suggestions are deterministic and test-covered.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.5.1","title":"Suggestions: data model + registry keyed by rule id","description":"## Objective

Define the suggestion data model and implement a registry lookup by stable rule id.

## Background

When DCG blocks a command, users need actionable guidance:
- What safer alternatives exist?
- How can they preview the effect first?
- How can they allowlist if intentional?

A well-designed suggestion system improves UX and reduces frustration.

## SuggestionKind Enum

```rust
/// Type of suggestion to help the user
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Serialize, Deserialize)]
#[serde(rename_all = "snake_case")]
pub enum SuggestionKind {
    /// "Run this first to preview the effect"
    /// e.g., "Run `git diff` before `git reset --hard`"
    PreviewFirst,

    /// "Use this safer alternative instead"
    /// e.g., "Use `git reset --soft` or `--mixed` instead of `--hard`"
    SaferAlternative,

    /// "Fix your workflow to avoid this situation"
    /// e.g., "Commit your changes before resetting"
    WorkflowFix,

    /// "Read the documentation for more context"
    /// e.g., "See: https://git-scm.com/docs/git-reset"
    Documentation,

    /// "How to allowlist this specific rule"
    /// e.g., "To allow: `dcg allow core.git:reset-hard --reason '...'`"
    AllowSafely,
}
```

## Suggestion Struct

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub struct Suggestion {
    /// Type of suggestion
    pub kind: SuggestionKind,

    /// Human-readable suggestion text
    pub text: String,

    /// Optional command the user can copy/paste
    #[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
    pub command: Option<String>,

    /// Optional URL for documentation
    #[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
    pub url: Option<String>,
}
```

## Registry Implementation

```rust
use std::sync::LazyLock;
use std::collections::HashMap;

/// Registry of suggestions keyed by rule_id (e.g., "core.git:reset-hard")
static SUGGESTION_REGISTRY: LazyLock<HashMap<&'static str, Vec<Suggestion>>> =
    LazyLock::new(|| {
        let mut m = HashMap::new();

        m.insert("core.git:reset-hard", vec![
            Suggestion {
                kind: SuggestionKind::PreviewFirst,
                text: "Run `git diff` and `git status` to see what would be lost".into(),
                command: Some("git diff && git status".into()),
                url: None,
            },
            Suggestion {
                kind: SuggestionKind::SaferAlternative,
                text: "Use `git reset --soft` or `--mixed` to preserve changes".into(),
                command: Some("git reset --soft HEAD~1".into()),
                url: None,
            },
            Suggestion {
                kind: SuggestionKind::WorkflowFix,
                text: "Consider using `git stash` to save changes temporarily".into(),
                command: Some("git stash".into()),
                url: None,
            },
        ]);

        // ... more rules
        m
    });

/// Look up suggestions for a rule
pub fn get_suggestions(rule_id: &str) -> Option<&'static [Suggestion]> {
    SUGGESTION_REGISTRY.get(rule_id).map(|v| v.as_slice())
}
```

## Output Formatting

### Hook Deny Output (stderr)
Show only top 1-3 suggestions to keep output bounded:

```
╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║  ⚠️  BLOCKED: git reset --hard HEAD~5                        ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║  Rule: core.git:reset-hard                                   ║
║  Reason: Hard reset can permanently lose uncommitted changes ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║  💡 Try instead:                                             ║
║  • Run `git diff` first to see what would be lost            ║
║  • Use `git reset --soft` to preserve changes                ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
```

### Explain Mode (JSON)
Include all suggestions in structured format:

```json
{
  "decision": "deny",
  "rule_id": "core.git:reset-hard",
  "reason": "Hard reset can permanently lose uncommitted changes",
  "suggestions": [
    {
      "kind": "preview_first",
      "text": "Run `git diff` and `git status` to see what would be lost",
      "command": "git diff && git status"
    },
    {
      "kind": "safer_alternative",
      "text": "Use `git reset --soft` or `--mixed` to preserve changes",
      "command": "git reset --soft HEAD~1"
    }
  ]
}
```

## Requirements

- Registry lookup is O(1) via HashMap
- Zero allocations on lookup (return static references)
- Suggestions are stable and deterministic (iteration order preserved)
- JSON output uses snake_case for kind values

## Acceptance Criteria

- Registry lookup is fast and allocation-light.
- JSON output for explain includes suggestions in a stable order.
- All SuggestionKind variants are used appropriately across rules.

## Tests

- Unit tests for registry lookup and ordering.
- Snapshot tests for JSON output format.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.5.2","title":"Suggestions: populate core.git + core.filesystem","description":"## Objective

Populate suggestions for the highest-traffic and highest-impact rules:

- `core.git:*`
- `core.filesystem:*`

## What to include

For each rule:

- a short "preview first" command when applicable
- a safer alternative when one exists
- the recommended allowlist command (rule-id based)

Examples (conceptual):

- `core.git:reset-hard`:
  - suggest `git stash` / `git diff` / `git reset --soft|--mixed`
- `core.git:push-force-*`:
  - suggest `--force-with-lease`
- `core.filesystem:rm-rf-*`:
  - suggest verifying path and using safer deletion flows when possible

## Acceptance criteria

- Every core rule has at least one suggestion.
- Suggestions are accurate and generic (no environment assumptions).

## Tests

- A "coverage" test that asserts all core destructive patterns have at least 1 suggestion.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.5.4","title":"Integrate suggestions into deny output and explain mode","description":"## Objective

Make suggestions visible where they matter:

- hook deny output (stderr) includes the top suggestion(s)
- `dcg explain` includes all suggestions (pretty/json/compact)

This task is also where we implement the **guided learning workflow** (hook-safe, non-interactive):

- on block, print copy/pasteable next steps (`dcg explain ...`, `dcg allowlist add ...`)
- no prompts; the hook must never wait for user input

## Requirements

### Hook deny output

- Must remain bounded; show only top 1–3 suggestions.
- Must include:
  - stable rule id (`pack_id:pattern_name`)
  - a short reason
  - at least one safe alternative suggestion (when available)
  - a copy/paste allowlist command example, e.g.:
    - `dcg allowlist add <rule_id> --reason "..." --project`
  - a copy/paste explain command:
    - `dcg explain "<command>"`

### Explain output

- Show all suggestions.
- JSON includes suggestions as structured objects.

## Acceptance criteria

- Deny output shows stable rule id + at least one suggestion.
- Deny output includes copy/paste learning commands (explain + allowlist add).
- Explain mode shows suggestions in all formats.

## Tests

- Snapshot tests for deny output (stderr) for a few core rules.
- Explain output tests include suggestions list.
- Regression test: deny output stays under a size cap even if a rule has many suggestions.

  - sort by severity then stable rule ID
  - cap list with a stable tie-breaker

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.6","title":"Implement dcg explain CLI subcommand","description":"## Objective

Implement the `dcg explain <command>` CLI subcommand that provides a full decision trace for any command.

## Background

When DCG blocks (or allows) a command, users and developers need to understand WHY:
- Which patterns matched?
- Which packs were evaluated?
- Was it blocked by a destructive pattern or allowed by a safe pattern?
- What context classification was applied?

The explain mode provides complete transparency into DCG's decision-making process.

## CLI Interface

```bash
# Basic usage
dcg explain "git reset --hard HEAD~5"

# Output format options
dcg explain "command" --format pretty   # Human-readable (default)
dcg explain "command" --format json     # Machine-readable
dcg explain "command" --format compact  # Single-line summary

# Verbose mode (include all evaluated packs, not just matching)
dcg explain "command" --verbose
```

## Decision Trace Structure

The explain output should show the complete decision pipeline:

```
╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║  DCG Decision Trace                                              ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║  Input: git reset --hard HEAD~5                                  ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║  1. Parse                                                        ║
║     └─ Tool: Bash                                                ║
║     └─ Command: git reset --hard HEAD~5                          ║
╠──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╣
║  2. Quick Reject                                                 ║
║     └─ Keywords found: git                                       ║
║     └─ Result: CONTINUE (keywords present)                       ║
╠──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╣
║  3. Normalization                                                ║
║     └─ Original: git reset --hard HEAD~5                         ║
║     └─ Normalized: git reset --hard HEAD~5                       ║
╠──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╣
║  4. Context Classification                                       ║
║     └─ Span[0..25]: Executed                                     ║
║     └─ No safe-string-arg context detected                       ║
╠──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╣
║  5. Heredoc Detection                                            ║
║     └─ Tier 1 trigger: NO                                        ║
║     └─ Result: SKIP heredoc analysis                             ║
╠──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╣
║  6. Pack Evaluation                                              ║
║     └─ Enabled packs: core.git, core.filesystem                  ║
║     └─ Pack: core.git                                            ║
║        ├─ Safe pattern check: NO MATCH                           ║
║        └─ Destructive pattern check:                             ║
║           └─ MATCH: reset-hard                                   ║
║              Pattern: git reset --hard                           ║
║              Reason: Hard reset can permanently lose commits     ║
╠──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╣
║  7. Decision                                                     ║
║     └─ Result: DENY                                              ║
║     └─ Rule ID: core.git:reset-hard                              ║
║     └─ Reason: Hard reset can permanently lose commits           ║
╠──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╣
║  8. Suggestions                                                  ║
║     └─ Run `git diff` first to see what would be lost            ║
║     └─ Use `git reset --soft` to preserve changes                ║
║     └─ To allow: dcg allow core.git:reset-hard --reason "..."    ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
```

## JSON Output Format

```json
{
  "input": {
    "raw": "git reset --hard HEAD~5",
    "tool": "Bash"
  },
  "trace": {
    "quick_reject": {
      "keywords_found": ["git"],
      "result": "continue"
    },
    "normalization": {
      "original": "git reset --hard HEAD~5",
      "normalized": "git reset --hard HEAD~5"
    },
    "context_classification": {
      "spans": [
        {"range": [0, 25], "kind": "executed"}
      ]
    },
    "heredoc_detection": {
      "tier1_triggered": false
    },
    "pack_evaluation": {
      "enabled_packs": ["core.git", "core.filesystem"],
      "matches": [
        {
          "pack_id": "core.git",
          "pattern_name": "reset-hard",
          "pattern": "git reset --hard",
          "reason": "Hard reset can permanently lose commits"
        }
      ]
    }
  },
  "decision": "deny",
  "rule_id": "core.git:reset-hard",
  "reason": "Hard reset can permanently lose commits",
  "suggestions": [
    {
      "kind": "preview_first",
      "text": "Run `git diff` first to see what would be lost"
    }
  ]
}
```

## Compact Output Format

```
DENY core.git:reset-hard "git reset --hard HEAD~5" -> Hard reset can permanently lose commits
```

## Implementation Notes

- Reuse the shared evaluator from 99e.3.1
- Add tracing/instrumentation hooks to capture each step
- Store trace in a `DecisionTrace` struct as evaluation proceeds
- Format the trace based on `--format` flag

## Performance

Explain mode is NOT on the hot path (user explicitly requests it), so:
- Can be slower than normal evaluation
- Can allocate for trace storage
- Should still complete in <100ms for typical commands

## Acceptance Criteria

- `dcg explain` works for all command types (simple, heredoc, pipes)
- Pretty format is human-readable and helps users understand decisions
- JSON format is valid and parseable by external tools
- Compact format is suitable for logging/scripting
- Suggestions are included in the output","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-07T21:23:51.003342694-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-07T21:55:30.329440887-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-07T21:55:30.329440887-05:00","close_reason":"Duplicate of git_safety_guard-1gt.1 + subtasks; superseded and split for better dependency tracking.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.6","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-1gt","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-07T21:23:51.004688188-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-484","title":"Coverage: no-mock unit tests + gating","description":"## Objective

Drive unit/integration test coverage toward **full functional coverage** while avoiding mocks/fakes. Emphasize real evaluator/packs behavior so failures reflect production reality.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-49s","title":"Implement heredoc syntax detection (regex quick-check)","description":"# Implement Heredoc Syntax Detection (Regex Quick-Check)

## Objective

Implement a fast regex-based detector that identifies commands potentially containing heredoc content.

## Background

Before we can scan heredoc content, we need to detect its presence. This is a two-stage approach:

1. Quick regex check (this task) - fast, may have false positives
2. Full extraction (separate task) - accurate, only runs if quick check triggers

## Implementation Details

### Quick-Check Patterns

The quick check should match common heredoc indicators:

```rust
// Patterns to detect heredoc presence
static HEREDOC_QUICK_PATTERNS: &[&str] = &[
    r"<<[-~]?\s*['\"]?\w+['\"]?",  // << EOF, <<- 'EOF', <<~ "END"
    r"<<<\s*['\"]",                 // <<< "here string"
    r"-c\s+['\"]",                  // python -c "code", bash -c 'code'
    r"-e\s+['\"]",                  // node -e "code", perl -e 'code'
];
```

### Integration Point

Add to the main processing pipeline after quick-reject but before pattern matching:

```rust
fn process_command(cmd: &str) -> Decision {
    // Existing quick-reject (no git/rm)
    if quick_reject(cmd) && global_quick_reject(cmd) {
        return Decision::Allow;
    }

    // NEW: Check for potential heredoc content
    if has_potential_heredoc(cmd) {
        if let Some(reason) = scan_heredoc_content(cmd) {
            return Decision::Deny(reason);
        }
    }

    // Continue with existing pattern matching...
}
```

### Performance Requirements

- Quick check must complete in < 0.5ms
- Should not allocate on the hot path
- Use lazy-compiled static patterns

### False Positive Tolerance

- Quick check can have false positives (triggers full scan unnecessarily)
- Quick check must NOT have false negatives (miss actual heredocs)
- Full scan will handle false positives gracefully

## Files to Modify

- src/packs/mod.rs or new src/heredoc/mod.rs
- src/main.rs (pipeline integration)

## Testing

- Test each heredoc variant is detected
- Test non-heredoc commands don't trigger
- Benchmark quick check performance

## Dependencies

- Design: heredoc detection strategy (git_safety_guard-o15)
- ADR approved (git_safety_guard-5ib)

## Acceptance Criteria

- All heredoc variants from design doc are detected by the quick-check.
- Quick check adds < 0.5ms to non-heredoc commands (validate via benchmark once perf harness exists).
- Zero false negatives in the tier-1 trigger test suite.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-e7m","title":"Integrate heredoc detection into main check pipeline","description":"## Objective

Integrate all heredoc detection components into the main dcg check pipeline, creating a seamless flow from command input to block/allow decision.

## Pipeline Architecture

```
JSON Input → Parse → Quick Reject → Normalize →
  → Heredoc Detection → [if heredoc found] →
    → Extract Content → Detect Language → Parse with ast-grep →
    → Apply Language Patterns → Block Decision
  → [no heredoc] → Existing Pattern Matching → Block Decision
```

## Integration Points

### 1. Entry Point Hook

Modify main.rs check flow to:

1. After quick reject passes, check for heredoc indicators
2. If heredoc found, branch to heredoc analysis path
3. If no heredoc, continue with existing pattern matching
4. Merge results from both paths

### 2. Heredoc Detection Integration

Insert heredoc detection before main pattern matching:

```rust
fn check_command(cmd: &str) -> CheckResult {
    // Quick reject (existing)
    if global_quick_reject(cmd) {
        return CheckResult::allowed();
    }

    // NEW: Heredoc detection
    if let Some(heredoc_result) = check_heredoc(cmd) {
        if heredoc_result.blocked {
            return heredoc_result;
        }
    }

    // Existing pattern matching
    REGISTRY.check_command(cmd, &enabled_packs)
}
```

### 3. Performance Budget

Total additional latency budget must be bounded.

- Tier 1 trigger: microseconds
- Tier 2 extraction: ~1ms typical
- Tier 3 AST/pattern: bounded by timeout (hard cap)

If AST analysis is too slow, fail-open (allow) in hook mode and emit a diagnostic.

### 4. Feature Flag / Pack integration

Add heredoc scanning to pack system:

- New pack: "heredoc" (or integrate into core)
- Can be enabled/disabled via configuration
- Default policy should be conservative (warn-first / strict mode opt-in)

### 5. Error Handling

Graceful degradation:

- AST engine unavailable → allow (hook) + diagnostic; optional fallback to simpler scanning
- AST timeout → allow (hook) + diagnostic
- Parse error → allow (hook) + diagnostic
- Unknown language → allow (hook) or generic high-signal checks only

## Configuration

New config options:

```toml
[heredoc]
enabled = true
timeout_ms = 50
fallback_on_error = true
languages = ["python", "bash", "javascript", "typescript", "ruby", "perl"]
```

## Testing Integration

Integration tests should cover:

- Normal commands (no heredoc) still work fast
- Heredoc commands get analyzed
- Pattern matches in heredocs block correctly
- Safe heredocs pass through
- Error paths handle gracefully

## Acceptance Criteria

- Heredoc path is executed only when Tier 1 triggers; non-heredoc commands remain fast.

-{"id":"git_safety_guard-erux","title":"Design user-friendly error messages for heredoc blocks","description":"# User-Friendly Error Messages for Heredoc Blocking

## Objective

When a heredoc command is blocked, provide clear, actionable error messages that help users understand WHY it was blocked and HOW to proceed.

## Current Error Format (for reference)

```
╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║  ⚠️  BLOCKED: Destructive Command Detected                   ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║  Command: git reset --hard HEAD~5                            ║
║  Reason:  Hard reset can permanently lose commits            ║
║  Pack:    core.git                                           ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
```

## Enhanced Error Format for Heredocs

```
╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║  ⚠️  BLOCKED: Destructive Pattern in Heredoc                 ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║  Language: Python                                            ║
║  Pattern:  os.system($CMD)                                   ║
║  Matched:  os.system("rm -rf /tmp/data")                    ║
║  Line:     3 of heredoc                                      ║
║  Reason:   Shell command execution via os.system             ║
║  Pack:     heredoc.python                                    ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║  Context:                                                    ║
║    1│ import os                                              ║
║    2│ path = "/tmp/data"                                    ║
║  ▶ 3│ os.system("rm -rf /tmp/data")  ← BLOCKED             ║
║    4│ print("done")                                         ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║  💡 Suggestions:                                             ║
║  • Use pathlib.Path.unlink() for single file deletion       ║
║  • Use shutil.rmtree() with explicit path validation        ║
║  • If intentional, use dcg allow --reason "..."             ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
```

## Error Message Components

### 1. Header

- Clear indication this is a BLOCK, not just a warning
- Specify it's a heredoc/inline script detection

### 2. Detection Details

- Language detected
- Pattern name that matched
- Actual matched text (truncated if long)
- Line number within heredoc
- Human-readable reason

### 3. Context Window

- Show 2-3 lines before and after the match
- Highlight the offending line with arrow
- Syntax highlighting if terminal supports it

### 4. Suggestions

- Language-specific safe alternatives
- How to override if intentional
- Link to documentation

## JSON Output Format

For Claude Code hook protocol:

```json
{
  "hookSpecificOutput": {
    "permissionDecision": "deny",
    "reason": "Heredoc contains destructive pattern: os.system with shell command",
    "details": {
      "detection_type": "heredoc",
      "language": "python",
      "pattern": "os.system($CMD)",
      "matched_text": "os.system(\"rm -rf /tmp/data\")",
      "line_in_heredoc": 3,
      "pack": "heredoc.python",
      "suggestions": [
        "Use pathlib.Path.unlink() for single file deletion",
        "Use shutil.rmtree() with explicit path validation"
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

## Suggestions Database

Maintain per-pattern suggestions:

```rust
static PATTERN_SUGGESTIONS: &[(&str, &[&str])] = &[
    ("os.system", &[
        "Use subprocess.run() with shell=False",
        "Use pathlib for file operations",
    ]),
    ("shutil.rmtree", &[
        "Validate path is within expected directory",
        "Use explicit allowlist of deletable paths",
    ]),
];
```

## Test Cases

- Verify all pattern types have suggestions
- Verify context window is correctly extracted
- Verify line numbers are accurate
- Verify JSON output is valid
- Verify color output is readable
- Verify truncation for long matches

## Acceptance Criteria

- When heredoc detection blocks, stderr output includes:
  - language
  - stable rule ID (pack_id:pattern_name)
  - a small context window with highlighted line
  - actionable next steps (explain/allowlist)
- Hook JSON denial remains valid and includes enough structured details for tooling.
- Output is safe for non-TTY environments (no ANSI when not a TTY).
- Unit tests cover at least:
  - context window extraction
  - truncation
  - JSON schema validity

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-f4f","title":"Implement ast-grep-core pattern matching layer","description":"# ast-grep-core Pattern Matching Layer

## Objective

Integrate ast-grep-core as a Rust library for structural pattern matching in heredoc/inline-script content.

## Architecture decision

Prefer **embedded ast-grep-core** over shelling out to a CLI, because:

- avoids per-invocation process spawn overhead
- keeps a single-binary distribution story
- enables richer match metadata (spans) for `dcg explain`

(Still subject to ADR confirmation; this task implements whichever embedded approach is chosen.)

## Dependencies to add (tentative)

```toml
[dependencies]
ast-grep-core = "0.40"
ast-grep-language = { version = "0.37", features = [
    "bash",
    "python",
    "javascript",
    "typescript",
    "ruby"
    # Note: Perl may not be available
] }
```

## API design

```rust
/// Pattern matcher using ast-grep-core
pub struct AstMatcher {
    patterns: HashMap<Language, Vec<CompiledPattern>>,
}

impl AstMatcher {
    /// Create matcher with default destructive patterns
    pub fn new() -> Self;

    /// Load patterns from config / registry
    pub fn from_config(config: &PatternConfig) -> Result<Self>;

    /// Match patterns against code (bounded)
    pub fn find_matches(&self, code: &str, language: Language) -> Result<Vec<PatternMatch>, MatchError>;
}

/// Result of pattern matching
pub struct PatternMatch {
    pub rule_id: String,             // stable: pack_id:pattern_name
    pub reason: String,
    pub matched_text_preview: String,
    pub start: usize,                // byte offset (or row/col)
    pub end: usize,
    pub line_number: usize,
    pub severity: Severity,          // ties into decision policy
}

pub enum Severity { Error, Warning, Info }
```

## Pattern definition format (example)

Patterns should be defined and reviewed under `git_safety_guard-6sg` (pattern library structure). The matching layer must be agnostic to *what* the patterns are, but must support:

- stable IDs
- per-pattern severity
- per-pattern suggestions (optional)

## Error handling / fail-open

In hook mode:

- Unsupported language → skip analysis (allow) + trace marker
- Parse error → skip analysis (allow) + trace marker
- Timeout → skip analysis (allow) + trace marker

In `dcg test` / `dcg explain` / `dcg scan`:

- return structured diagnostics (not silent skip)

## Performance targets (initial)

- Pattern compilation: one-time at startup (bounded)
- Parse: <2ms for typical heredoc sizes
- Match: <1ms typical
- Total AST path: bounded by a hard timeout

## Acceptance Criteria

- Implements an embedded AST matcher that can:
  - parse at least one supported language
  - match at least one simple pattern
  - return match spans + line number
- Enforces a hard timeout for AST work and fails open in hook mode on timeout.
- Produces match metadata compatible with:
  - `dcg explain` highlighting (matched span + preview)
  - rule attribution (stable rule_id)
  - decision policy (severity)
- Includes unit tests for:
  - a positive match
  - a negative (safe) snippet
  - an unsupported-language path
  - a timeout path (if testable)

- prefer composite/high-signal rules (AST match + literal destructive payload) over broad matches (e.g., `exec($VAR)`)

### Tests

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-fpim","title":"Implement false positive reduction for string arguments","description":"# False Positive Reduction Implementation

## Problem

Current regex patterns match dangerous strings even in safe contexts:
- `bd create --description="This blocks rm -rf"` → BLOCKED (wrong!)
- `git commit -m "Fix rm -rf pattern"` → BLOCKED (wrong!)
- `grep "rm -rf" patterns.txt` → BLOCKED (wrong!)

## Solution

Parse command structure to identify execution context before applying destructive patterns.

## Implementation

### 1. Safe Command Registry

```rust
static SAFE_STRING_ARG_COMMANDS: &[(&str, &[&str])] = &[
    ("bd", &["--description", "--title", "-d", "-t"]),
    ("git", &["commit -m", "tag -m", "notes add -m"]),
    ("grep", &["-e", "--regexp"]),
    ("rg", &["-e", "--regexp"]),
    ("echo", &[]),  // all args are strings
    ("printf", &[]),
];
```

### 2. Execution Context Detection

```rust
enum ExecutionContext {
    Direct,           // Command itself is executed
    StringArgument,   // Inside quoted string argument to safe command
    HeredocBody,      // Inside heredoc (needs Tier 2 analysis)
    PipeTarget,       // Right side of pipe (executed)
    CommandSubst,     // Inside $() or backticks (executed)
}

fn detect_execution_context(cmd: &str, position: usize) -> ExecutionContext;
```

### 3. Integration Point

Modify main check flow:

```rust
fn check_command(cmd: &str) -> CheckResult {
    // Quick reject (existing)
    if global_quick_reject(cmd) {
        return CheckResult::allowed();
    }

    // NEW: Check if dangerous patterns are in safe context
    if is_safe_string_context(cmd) {
        return CheckResult::allowed();
    }

    // Continue with heredoc detection and pattern matching...
}
```

## Test Cases

### Must ALLOW (false positive prevention)
- `bd create --description="Pattern blocks rm -rf commands"`
- `git commit -m "Fix git reset --hard detection"`
- `grep "rm -rf" src/patterns.rs`
- `echo "example: git push --force"`
- `rg -e "DROP TABLE" --type sql`

### Must BLOCK (true positives preserved)
- `rm -rf /tmp/*`
- `git reset --hard HEAD~5`
- `bash -c "rm -rf /"`
- `echo "hi" | bash -c "rm -rf /"`

## Performance

- < 100 microseconds additional overhead
- Simple string prefix matching for command detection
- No regex needed for this layer

## Dependencies

- Depends on: False positive design (2cu)

## Acceptance Criteria

- The documented false-positive examples are allowed in hook mode and `dcg test`.
- True-positive executions are still blocked, including destructive strings inside:
  - `bash -c ...`
  - `sh -c ...`
  - `node -e ...` (where the code executes)
  - command substitution (`$(...)` / backticks)
- Context classification is conservative:
  - only skips matching inside explicitly-registered safe string arguments
  - does not treat unknown flags/commands as safe
- Performance overhead is bounded (target < 100µs additional overhead on typical allow-path commands).
","notes":"## Objective

+# Heredoc Pattern Authoring Guide
+
+This document describes how heredoc and inline-script patterns are defined, tested,
+and allowlisted. It is intended for contributors and power users.
+
+## Overview
+
+Heredoc detection protects against destructive commands embedded in heredocs,
+here-strings, and inline interpreter flags (for example, `python -c`). The
+implementation uses a tiered pipeline (see `docs/adr-001-heredoc-scanning.md`)
+that only evaluates heredoc patterns when a heredoc or inline script is detected.
+
+Stable rule IDs are required for allowlisting. The naming convention is:
+
+```
+heredoc.<language>.<operation>
+```
+
+Example:
+
+```
+heredoc.python.shutil_rmtree
+```
+
+Rule IDs are the keys used in allowlists and explain output.
+
+Pipeline (simplified):
+
+```
+command
+  -> quick reject
+  -> heredoc trigger
+  -> extract content + detect language
+  -> ast match (language patterns)
+  -> decision (allow/warn/deny)
+```
+
+## Pattern Syntax
+
+Heredoc patterns are authored using ast-grep pattern syntax (as implemented by
+`ast-grep-core`). Common conventions used in this repo:
+
+- `$$$` matches any subtree.
+- `$X` captures a single AST node.
+- Patterns are language-specific and only evaluated on code parsed for that
+  language.
+
+Examples:
+
+- `shutil.rmtree($$$)`
+- `child_process.execSync($$$)`
+- `exec.Command($$$).Run()`
+
+Perl patterns are handled via targeted regex scanning of string literals and
+shell payloads (see `src/ast_matcher.rs`).
+
+## Where Patterns Live
+
+The built-in pattern inventory is defined in:
+
+- `src/ast_matcher.rs` (`default_patterns()` and Perl scanners)
+
+Suggestions for some patterns are mapped in:
+
+- `src/suggestions.rs`
+
+The high-level design and rationale are documented in:
+
+- `docs/adr-001-heredoc-scanning.md`
+- `docs/pattern-library-design.md`
+
+## Adding New Patterns
+
+1. **Choose a stable rule ID** (`heredoc.<language>.<operation>`). Do not rename
+   existing IDs; deprecate instead.
+2. **Add a `CompiledPattern::new(...)` entry** in `src/ast_matcher.rs` for
+   language-specific AST matches, or extend the Perl scanners for regex-based
+   matching.
+3. **Provide a concise reason** (short, human-readable, under 100 chars).
+4. **Set severity** (Critical / High / Medium / Low). Consider false-positive
+   risk and catastrophic targets.
+5. **Add a suggestion** when a safer alternative exists.
+6. **Add tests** (positive and negative fixtures) in `src/ast_matcher.rs`.
+7. **Update docs** (this file and any relevant design notes).
+
+## Adding a New Language
+
+1. Add the language to `ScriptLanguage` in `src/heredoc.rs`.
+2. Map string aliases in `src/config.rs` heredoc language parsing.
+3. Map to an AST language in `src/ast_matcher.rs` (`script_language_to_ast_lang`).
+4. Add default patterns in `src/ast_matcher.rs`.
+5. Add suggestions in `src/suggestions.rs` (if applicable).
+6. Add positive and negative fixtures in `src/ast_matcher.rs`.
+
+## Pattern Inventory (Current Defaults)
+
+This list is derived from `src/ast_matcher.rs` and reflects the current
+built-in rule IDs. Use these IDs for allowlisting and tests.
+
+### Bash
+
+| Rule ID | Pattern | Reason |
+| --- | --- | --- |
+| `heredoc.bash.rm_r` | `rm -r $$$` | `rm -r` recursively deletes |
+| `heredoc.bash.rm_rf` | `rm -rf $$$` | `rm -rf` recursively deletes files/directories |
+| `heredoc.bash.git_reset_hard` | `git reset --hard` | discards uncommitted changes |
+| `heredoc.bash.git_clean_fd` | `git clean -fd` | deletes untracked files |
+
+### Go
+
+| Rule ID | Pattern | Reason |
+| --- | --- | --- |
+| `heredoc.go.os_remove` | `os.Remove($$$)` | deletes files |
+| `heredoc.go.os_removeall` | `os.RemoveAll($$$)` | recursively deletes directories |
+| `heredoc.go.exec_command` | `exec.Command($$$)` | executes shell commands |
+| `heredoc.go.exec_command_run` | `exec.Command($$$).Run()` | executes shell commands |
+| `heredoc.go.exec_command_output` | `exec.Command($$$).Output()` | executes shell commands |
+| `heredoc.go.exec_command_combined_output` | `exec.Command($$$).CombinedOutput()` | executes shell commands |
+
+### JavaScript (Node)
+
+| Rule ID | Pattern | Reason |
+| --- | --- | --- |
+| `heredoc.javascript.fs_rm` | `fs.rm($$$)` | deletes files/directories |
+| `heredoc.javascript.fs_rmdir` | `fs.rmdir($$$)` | deletes directories |
+| `heredoc.javascript.fs_rmsync` | `fs.rmSync($$$)` | deletes files/directories |
+| `heredoc.javascript.fs_rmdirsync` | `fs.rmdirSync($$$)` | deletes directories |
+| `heredoc.javascript.fs_unlink` | `fs.unlink($$$)` | deletes files |
+| `heredoc.javascript.fs_unlinksync` | `fs.unlinkSync($$$)` | deletes files |
+| `heredoc.javascript.fspromises_rm` | `fsPromises.rm($$$)` | deletes files/directories |
+| `heredoc.javascript.fspromises_rmdir` | `fsPromises.rmdir($$$)` | deletes directories |
+| `heredoc.javascript.execsync` | `child_process.execSync($$$)` | executes shell commands |
+| `heredoc.javascript.require_execsync` | `require('child_process').execSync($$$)` | executes shell commands |
+| `heredoc.javascript.spawnsync` | `child_process.spawnSync($$$)` | executes shell commands |
+
+### TypeScript
+
+| Rule ID | Pattern | Reason |
+| --- | --- | --- |
+| `heredoc.typescript.fs_rm` | `fs.rm($$$)` | deletes files/directories |
+| `heredoc.typescript.fs_rmdir` | `fs.rmdir($$$)` | deletes directories |
+| `heredoc.typescript.fs_rmsync` | `fs.rmSync($$$)` | deletes files/directories |
+| `heredoc.typescript.fs_rmdirsync` | `fs.rmdirSync($$$)` | deletes directories |
+| `heredoc.typescript.fs_unlink` | `fs.unlink($$$)` | deletes files |
+| `heredoc.typescript.fs_unlinksync` | `fs.unlinkSync($$$)` | deletes files |
+| `heredoc.typescript.fspromises_rm` | `fsPromises.rm($$$)` | deletes files/directories |
+| `heredoc.typescript.fspromises_rmdir` | `fsPromises.rmdir($$$)` | deletes directories |
+| `heredoc.typescript.execsync` | `child_process.execSync($$$)` | executes shell commands |
+| `heredoc.typescript.require_execsync` | `require('child_process').execSync($$$)` | executes shell commands |
+| `heredoc.typescript.spawnsync` | `child_process.spawnSync($$$)` | executes shell commands |
+| `heredoc.typescript.deno_remove` | `Deno.remove($$$)` | deletes files/directories |
+
+### Python
+
+| Rule ID | Pattern | Reason |
+| --- | --- | --- |
+| `heredoc.python.shutil_rmtree` | `shutil.rmtree($$$)` | recursively deletes directories |
+| `heredoc.python.os_remove` | `os.remove($$$)` | deletes files |
+| `heredoc.python.os_rmdir` | `os.rmdir($$$)` | deletes directories |
+| `heredoc.python.os_unlink` | `os.unlink($$$)` | deletes files |
+| `heredoc.python.pathlib_unlink` | `pathlib.Path($$$).unlink($$$)` and `Path($$$).unlink($$$)` | deletes files |
+| `heredoc.python.pathlib_rmdir` | `pathlib.Path($$$).rmdir($$$)` and `Path($$$).rmdir($$$)` | deletes directories |
+| `heredoc.python.subprocess_run` | `subprocess.run($$$)` | executes shell commands |
+| `heredoc.python.subprocess_call` | `subprocess.call($$$)` | executes shell commands |
+| `heredoc.python.subprocess_popen` | `subprocess.Popen($$$)` | spawns shell processes |
+| `heredoc.python.os_system` | `os.system($$$)` | executes shell commands |
+| `heredoc.python.os_popen` | `os.popen($$$)` | executes shell commands |
+
+### Ruby
+
+| Rule ID | Pattern | Reason |
+| --- | --- | --- |
+| `heredoc.ruby.file_delete` | `File.delete($$$)` | deletes files |
+| `heredoc.ruby.file_unlink` | `File.unlink($$$)` | deletes files |
+| `heredoc.ruby.dir_delete` | `Dir.delete($$$)` | deletes directories |
+| `heredoc.ruby.dir_rmdir` | `Dir.rmdir($$$)` | deletes directories |
+| `heredoc.ruby.fileutils_rm` | `FileUtils.rm($$$)` | deletes files |
+| `heredoc.ruby.fileutils_remove` | `FileUtils.remove($$$)` | deletes files |
+| `heredoc.ruby.fileutils_remove_dir` | `FileUtils.remove_dir($$$)` | deletes directories |
+| `heredoc.ruby.fileutils_rm_rf` | `FileUtils.rm_rf($$$)` | recursively deletes directories |
+| `heredoc.ruby.system` | `system($$$)` | executes shell commands |
+| `heredoc.ruby.exec` | `exec($$$)` | replaces process with shell command |
+| `heredoc.ruby.kernel_system` | `Kernel.system($$$)` | executes shell commands |
+| `heredoc.ruby.kernel_exec` | `Kernel.exec($$$)` | replaces process with shell command |
+| `heredoc.ruby.open3_capture3` | `Open3.capture3($$$)` | executes shell commands |
+| `heredoc.ruby.open3_popen3` | `Open3.popen3($$$)` | executes shell commands |
+| `heredoc.ruby.backticks` | `` `$$$` `` | executes shell commands |
+
+### Perl
+
+Perl scanning uses targeted regexes plus shell-payload analysis. The following
+rule IDs are emitted:
+
+- `heredoc.perl.file_path.rmtree`
+- `heredoc.perl.file_path.<fn_name>` (for `File::Path::<fn_name>`)
+- `heredoc.perl.unlink`
+- `heredoc.perl.rmdir`
+- `heredoc.perl.system.<suffix>`
+- `heredoc.perl.exec.<suffix>`
+- `heredoc.perl.backticks.<suffix>`
+- `heredoc.perl.qx.<suffix>`
+
+Supported `<suffix>` values (from shell-payload detection):
+
+- `git_reset_hard`
+- `git_clean_fd`
+- `rm_rf`
+- `rm_rf_catastrophic`
+
+## Derived Rule IDs
+
+Some patterns refine their rule IDs based on detected arguments:
+
+- For JavaScript/TypeScript `fs.*` and `fsPromises.*` patterns, a literal
+  catastrophic path appends `.catastrophic` to the rule ID
+  (example: `heredoc.javascript.fs_rmsync.catastrophic`).
+- For TypeScript `deno_remove`, a catastrophic path appends `.catastrophic`.
+- For Ruby `FileUtils`/`File`/`Dir` patterns, catastrophic paths append
+  `.catastrophic`.
+- For Ruby `system`/`exec`/`Open3`/backticks and Perl shell calls, literal
+  payloads produce rule IDs with suffixes such as `.rm_rf` and
+  `.rm_rf_catastrophic`.
+
+All derived rule IDs are valid allowlist targets.
+
+## Limitations and False Positive Notes
+
+- Shell execution helpers (for example, `execSync`, `system`, `os.system`) only
+  escalate to high-severity decisions when a literal payload is detected. Dynamic
+  payloads are warn-only to avoid false positives.
+- Some file deletion APIs are refined at match time. Non-recursive or
+  non-catastrophic paths may result in warn-only severity.
+- Patterns are evaluated only for supported languages and only when heredoc
+  triggers are detected. Non-heredoc destructive code outside the supported
+  languages is out of scope.
+
+## Testing Requirements
+
+Add tests under `src/ast_matcher.rs`:
+
+- At least one positive and one negative fixture per new rule.
+- Ensure comments/strings do not match (false positive guard).
+- Include catastrophic-path fixtures when applicable.
+
+Run:
+
+```
+cargo test ast_matcher
+```
+

+# Security Notes: Heredoc Detection
+
+This document describes the threat model, assumptions, and incident response
+for heredoc and inline-script scanning.
+
+## Threat Model
+
+Heredoc scanning is designed to catch destructive operations hidden inside
+embedded scripts, including:
+
+- Heredocs: `<<EOF ... EOF`
+- Here-strings: `<<< "..."`
+- Inline interpreter flags: `python -c`, `bash -c`, `node -e`
+- Piped scripts: `cat <<EOF | bash`
+
+The goal is to prevent accidental or automated destructive actions that the
+outer command does not reveal.
+
+## What It Protects Against
+
+- Destructive filesystem commands inside scripts (for example, recursive delete)
+- Destructive git operations embedded in scripts (for example, `git reset --hard`)
+- Shell execution helpers in scripting languages (for example, `os.system`,
+  `child_process.execSync`, `Kernel.system`)
+
+## Out of Scope
+
+- General malware detection
+- Exploits that do not rely on heredocs or inline script payloads
+- Non-shell destructive operations outside the supported language set
+- Arbitrary interpreter behavior that is not represented in the AST patterns
+
+These limits keep runtime overhead small and false positives manageable.
+
+## Fail-Open Behavior
+
+In hook mode, heredoc scanning is **fail-open** by design:
+
+- Parse errors or timeouts result in ALLOW
+- Unknown languages result in ALLOW
+
+This prevents the hook from breaking legitimate workflows. Diagnostic markers
+are emitted so that `dcg explain` or logs can surface the failure.
+
+## Performance Budgets
+
+The heredoc pipeline is strictly bounded:
+
+- Tier 1 trigger: <100us
+- Tier 2 extraction: <1ms typical, 50ms max
+- Tier 3 AST match: <5ms typical, 20ms max
+
+When budgets are exceeded, the system fails open and records a diagnostic.
+
+## Bypass Considerations
+
+Heredoc scanning is not intended to be a perfect malware detector. Known
+limitations include:
+
+- Obfuscated payloads that evade AST parsing or use unsupported languages
+- Dynamic command construction that cannot be resolved to literal payloads
+- Non-standard interpreters or runtime-generated code
+
+Mitigations:
+
+- Favor stable rule IDs and allowlisting for known-safe cases
+- Keep patterns narrowly scoped to avoid broad false positives
+- Expand language support and pattern coverage based on real-world feedback
+
+## Incident Response
+
+### If a safe command is blocked
+
+1. Run `dcg explain` on the command or use the printed rule ID.
+2. Add a scoped allowlist entry with a reason.
+3. Consider reducing scope (project allowlist vs user/system allowlist).
+
+### If a dangerous command is allowed
+
+1. Capture the command text and environment context.
+2. File a security issue with the rule ID or gap description.
+3. Add or refine a heredoc pattern and tests.
+
+## Reporting
+
+Security issues should be reported via GitHub issues with:
+
+- The command and any heredoc payload (redacted as needed)
+- The language detected (if any)
+- The observed behavior (blocked or allowed)
+- Expected behavior and rationale
+
